I. Summary of Opinions:
"Ongoing exposure in restricted counties" — implies active targeting, but Brown admits she cannot verify geo-targeting settings without internal data. - The 1998 judgement is regarding naming practices and advertising in specific locales. If Dunham & Jones were advertising in those areas it would be clearly provable. The fact that she cannot product a screenshot of an ad while in one of those areas invalidates her claim. Furthermore, the fact that Dunham & Jones REDIRECTS their domain in those areas to a completely different domain is clear evidence that they are going above and beyond to try and please Dunnam & Dunnam. In no other world would a successful business with a 20+ year established domain, redirect their primary domain for a small handful of it's locations. 

"Continued algorithmic association with the Dunnam & Dunnam brand" — conflates organic search engine behavior (which defendants don't control) with intentional conduct. - The closest Dunham & Jones could possibly come to this is if they were to try to "influence" search rankings by including the "Dunnam & Dunnam" brand in the context of their website and it's pages. This has never been true, and if it were true, it would be verifiable by a simple site:dunhamlaw.com "dunnam" Google search, or furthermore by scraping the dunhamlaw.com website with a tool such as Screaming Frog, which Caryn claims to use. All text content, meta data, site architecture, and technology used by dunhamlaw.com is visible to the public. If there were, or ever had been any instance of them trying to manipulate search rankings for the "Dunnam & Dunnam" brand, it would be public to prove using simply a browser. 

Furthermore "Compliance assertions cannot be verified without defendants' internal documentation" — frames the absence of evidence as evidence of non-compliance. Which she seems to do often.
II. Assignment and Scope: Brown repeatedly "refrains from assuming compliance" — effectively presumes guilt and shifts the burden of proof to the defendants. If any of her claims were true it would be very easy for her to produce proof. If it's visible to Google, it's visible to you.
III. Sources, Methods, and Limitations: Brown does not name the specific third-party tools used (e.g., Ahrefs, SEMrush, SimilarWeb) — making her methodology non-reproducible. No crawl parameters disclosed: user-agent, crawl date, deduplication method, or scope. She only retroactively acknowledges this issue in Section XI (p. 12). All of these tools provide downloadable reports. If using them surfaced her claims, she should provide the associated report showing proof.
IV. Findings A — DunhamLaw.com:
"Dunham" is the defendants' actual legal name — the 1998 judgment prohibits specific name formulations like "Dunham Law Firm" in specific counties, not the use of the surname "Dunham" generally. "DunhamLaw.com" vs. "Dunham Law Firm" is a critical distinction.

Ranking for "dunnam & dunnam" queries is organic search engine behavior — defendants have no control over Google's algorithm choosing to surface DunhamLaw.com for "Dunnam" queries due to name similarity.

Keyword counts (450 mentions of "Waco criminal attorney") - Having keywords on their website is not a violation of the judgement and is not an indicator that they are explicitly advertising using the brand names not allowed in those areas. They are not in any way connected. Dunham & Jones is allowed to operate in those locations and it is only logical that they would mention them on their website. 

Brown concedes she cannot verify paid search targeting but still implies violation through "brand-adjacent visibility" language. - If they were running paid search ads in those areas, you would be able to see and screenshot them. Furthermore, the 1998 judgement prohibits them from advertising in those areas USING THE PROHIBITED NAMES, not from advertising under the new name they assumed as a result of the 1998 ruling.
V. Findings B — DunhamJones.com:
Ranking for "Waco criminal defense" queries is not itself a violation — the judgment restricts certain names, not the right to practice or advertise under non-prohibited names.

Finding no paid activity but arguing it "doesn't establish compliance" is unfalsifiable reasoning — no evidence could ever satisfy this standard.
VI. Developments Since August 2025:
She again presumes non-compliance because she can't confirm compliance from public data alone.

The "aggregate footprint" across domains is not a recognized legal or technical standard for measuring compliance. Nor is using dunhamlaw.com prohibited in the 1998 judgement. She's lucky that Dunham & Jones redirects their domain in those areas to dunhamjones.com - they don't have to in my opinion.
VII. Intent, Ability to Comply, and Post: Brown is offering legal conclusions about intent and willfulness — this exceeds the scope of a technical expert and invades the province of the fact-finder. Furthermore, she has no facts to support her claims. If any of them were true she would have hard evidence. The chatbot anecdote is self-admittedly non-reproducible and irrelevant — defendants don't control third-party AI chatbot behavior. "Algorithmic ambiguity" from overlapping names is an inherent consequence of two firms with similar surnames — not evidence of misconduct. Neither party controls how Google ranks results for ANY query.
VIII. Technical Simplicity of Compliance:
This section presumes non-compliance and then argues compliance would be easy — but never establishes what specific violations need remediation. 

"Disabling DNS for prohibited domains" — which domains does she consider "prohibited"? No domains in use by Dunham & Jones are prohibited. 

Negative keyword implementation for "protected marks" - Dunham & Jones is not and has never advertised against the  "Dunnam & Dunnam" brand name or terms. EVEN IF THEY HAD it would not be in violation of the judgement, the law, not even Google Ads policy. Google allows competitors to bid against each other's brand terms. Dunham & Jones has not and does not do this.
IX–X. Verification Matrix & Recommended:
The matrix frames everything as "proof required from defendants" — Brown could have conducted more thorough public analysis using industry-standard tools (SEMrush, SimilarWeb, Google Ads Transparency Center) but apparently did not.

Several items Brown claims are "not publicly verifiable" (metadata, domain redirects, paid ad presence) are in fact partially or fully verifiable through public tools.

It appears Brown either does not know how to use the tools she claims to use that would explicitly reveal all her claims to be true, or the claims are simply not true and use of these very tools would prove it.
XI. Quantitative Indicators & Methodolog:
Brown herself identifies all the methodological standards she failed to meet — this is a significant admission that her own counts are unreliable.

"Approximate values" and "descriptive indicators" cannot support the definitive conclusions she draws elsewhere in the report (e.g., "no material retreat").
XIII. Corrective Advertising:
No methodology, market data, or comparable cases cited to support the dollar figures. For a regional Waco market, these numbers are vastly inflated.
2.
Brown charges $125/hour but proposes $12,000–$20,000/year for "monitoring" — this appears designed to create an ongoing revenue stream for her own firm.
3.
"Associations do not self-correct" is misleading — search engine results change constantly, and historical SEO signals decay naturally over weeks/months, not the "multi-year" period she asserts.
4.
The entire corrective advertising section presumes proven violations and proven damages — neither has been established.
5.
"Paid search corrective ads" of $25K–$50K/year in a regional market like Waco is orders of magnitude beyond what would be needed for any legitimate clarification campaign.
